title: Getting Started with .NET MAUI on Linux: Your First OpenMaui Application
subtitle: Build native Linux desktop applications with .NET MAUI using the OpenMaui platform — bringing cross-platform development to the Linux ecosystem.
pullQuotes: OpenMaui achieves true cross-platform compatibility by implementing MAUI handler interfaces that map MAUI controls to Skia views, ensuring existing MAUI applications can target Linux with minimal or no code changes.
With 217+ passing unit tests and support for both X11 and Wayland display servers, OpenMaui brings enterprise-grade reliability to Linux desktop development.
The platform uses SkiaSharp for rendering with GPU acceleration and automatic software fallback, ensuring your applications run smoothly across diverse Linux desktop environments.

sections.content:
The .NET MAUI (Multi-platform App UI) framework has revolutionized cross-platform development for Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android — but until now, Linux desktop has been conspicuously absent from the supported platforms. **OpenMaui** changes that.

Developed by MarketAlly Pte Ltd under the leadership of David H. Friedel Jr., OpenMaui.Controls.Linux is a community-driven implementation that brings full .NET MAUI support to Linux desktop environments. Whether you're targeting GNOME, KDE, XFCE, or other desktop environments, OpenMaui provides native integration with both X11 and Wayland display servers.

**Why choose OpenMaui for Linux development?**

- **100% MAUI API compatibility**: Your existing MAUI knowledge transfers directly — same controls, same patterns, same XAML
- **Native Linux integration**: Leverages freedesktop.org standards including xdg-desktop-portal, D-Bus notifications, and AT-SPI2 accessibility
- **SkiaSharp rendering**: Hardware-accelerated 2D graphics with automatic GPU/CPU fallback for maximum compatibility
- **Comprehensive control library**: 35+ fully implemented controls including advanced widgets like CollectionView, CarouselView, Shell, and WebView
- **Production-ready**: 217+ passing unit tests covering handlers, rendering pipeline, and platform services

OpenMaui targets .NET 10 and MAUI 10.0.41, ensuring you're working with the latest framework features. The platform uses a custom rendering stack with SkiaSharp 3.x for graphics, HarfBuzzSharp 8.x for advanced text shaping (including emoji and CJK character support), and native integration with GTK3, X11, and Wayland.

Whether you're building business applications, developer tools, or consumer software, OpenMaui enables you to reach Linux users with the same codebase you're already using for other platforms.

Before diving into OpenMaui development, you'll need to set up your Linux development environment with the necessary tools and dependencies.

### Required Software

**1. .NET SDK 10 or later**

OpenMaui targets .NET 10, so you'll need the corresponding SDK installed:

```bash
# Download and install .NET 10 SDK
wget https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh -O dotnet-install.sh
chmod +x dotnet-install.sh
./dotnet-install.sh --channel 10.0
```

Verify your installation:

```bash
dotnet --version
# Should output 10.x.x or later
```

**2. .NET MAUI Workload**

Install the MAUI workload for .NET:

```bash
dotnet workload install maui
```

### System Dependencies

OpenMaui relies on several native Linux libraries for rendering, windowing, and platform services. Install these using your distribution's package manager:

**Ubuntu/Debian:**

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y \
  libgtk-3-dev \
  libx11-dev \
  libxrandr-dev \
  libfontconfig1-dev \
  libskia-dev \
  libharfbuzz-dev \
  libwebkit2gtk-4.0-dev \
  libsecret-1-dev \
  libnotify-dev \
  libibus-1.0-dev
```

**Fedora/RHEL:**

```bash
sudo dnf install -y \
  gtk3-devel \
  libX11-devel \
  libXrandr-devel \
  fontconfig-devel \
  skia-devel \
  harfbuzz-devel \
  webkit2gtk3-devel \
  libsecret-devel \
  libnotify-devel \
  ibus-devel
```

**Arch Linux:**

```bash
sudo pacman -S gtk3 libx11 libxrandr fontconfig skia harfbuzz webkit2gtk libsecret libnotify ibus
```

### Optional but Recommended

- **Visual Studio Code**: Lightweight editor with excellent .NET support
- **Visual Studio 2022** (via WSL): Full IDE experience with OpenMaui VSIX extension
- **xdg-desktop-portal**: For native file picker dialogs
- **zenity** or **kdialog**: Fallback dialog tools

### NuGet Dependencies

The OpenMaui package automatically includes these dependencies:

- **Microsoft.Maui.Controls** (10.0.41)
- **SkiaSharp** (3.119.2) with NativeAssets.Linux
- **HarfBuzzSharp** (8.3.1.3)
- **Svg.Skia** (3.4.1) for icon generation

You don't need to install these manually — they'll be pulled in when you reference the OpenMaui.Controls.Linux package.

### Verifying Your Setup

Once everything is installed, verify that the key libraries are available:

```bash
pkg-config --modversion gtk+-3.0
pkg-config --modversion x11
pkg-config --modversion harfbuzz
```

You're now ready to install the OpenMaui templates and create your first project!

OpenMaui provides project templates that streamline the creation of new Linux MAUI applications. These templates come in two flavors: code-based and XAML-based.

### Installing from NuGet

The easiest way to get started is to install the OpenMaui templates package:

```bash
dotnet new install OpenMaui.Templates.Linux
```

This installs two project templates:

- **openmaui-linux**: Code-based template with programmatic UI construction
- **openmaui-linux-xaml**: XAML-based template with declarative UI markup

Verify the installation:

```bash
dotnet new list | grep openmaui
```

You should see output similar to:

```
openmaui-linux          OpenMaui Linux App              C#        MAUI/Linux/Desktop
openmaui-linux-xaml     OpenMaui Linux App (XAML)       C#        MAUI/Linux/Desktop/XAML
```

### Template Features

Both templates provide:

- **Pre-configured project files** with correct target framework (net10.0) and MAUI SDK
- **Platform initialization** with `UseMauiApp<>()` and Linux platform configuration
- **Sample UI** demonstrating basic controls and layout
- **Launch profiles** for debugging with Visual Studio Code and Visual Studio
- **NuGet package references** including OpenMaui.Controls.Linux (v10.0.41.1)

### Visual Studio Extension (Optional)

If you're using Visual Studio 2022 on Windows with WSL or remote Linux development, install the OpenMaui VSIX extension:

1. Download the extension from the Visual Studio Marketplace
2. Double-click the `.vsix` file to install
3. Restart Visual Studio
4. Create a new project and search for "OpenMaui Linux App"

The extension adds:

- **Project templates** integrated into the New Project dialog
- **Launch profiles** for WSL, local, and remote debugging
- **IntelliSense support** for OpenMaui-specific APIs

### Updating Templates

As OpenMaui evolves, you'll want to keep your templates up to date:

```bash
# Uninstall old version
dotnet new uninstall OpenMaui.Templates.Linux

# Install latest version
dotnet new install OpenMaui.Templates.Linux
```

### What's Next?

With the templates installed, you're ready to create your first OpenMaui application. In the next section, we'll scaffold a new project and explore the generated structure.

Let's create your first OpenMaui application and get it running on Linux. We'll use the XAML template for this walkthrough, as it demonstrates the full power of declarative UI development.

### Scaffolding the Project

Open your terminal and navigate to your desired workspace directory:

```bash
mkdir ~/MauiProjects
cd ~/MauiProjects

# Create a new XAML-based OpenMaui project
dotnet new openmaui-linux-xaml -n MyFirstMauiApp
cd MyFirstMauiApp
```

The template creates a complete project structure with:

- `MyFirstMauiApp.csproj` - Project file with MAUI SDK and OpenMaui references
- `MauiProgram.cs` - Application entry point and service configuration
- `App.xaml` / `App.xaml.cs` - Application-level resources and initialization
- `MainPage.xaml` / `MainPage.xaml.cs` - Default page with sample UI
- `Platforms/Linux/` - Linux-specific platform code

### Examining the Project File

Open `MyFirstMauiApp.csproj` to see the configuration:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <UseMaui>true</UseMaui>
    <SingleProject>true</SingleProject>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Maui.Controls" Version="10.0.41" />
    <PackageReference Include="OpenMaui.Controls.Linux" Version="10.0.41.1" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

### Understanding MauiProgram.cs

The `MauiProgram.cs` file is where OpenMaui gets initialized:

```csharp
using Microsoft.Maui.Hosting;
using OpenMaui.Platform.Linux;

public static class MauiProgram
{
    public static MauiApp CreateMauiApp()
    {
        var builder = MauiApp.CreateBuilder();
        builder
            .UseMauiApp<App>()
            .UseLinuxPlatform(options =>
            {
                // Auto-detect X11 or Wayland
                options.DisplayServer = DisplayServer.Auto;
                
                // Enable GPU acceleration
                options.EnableGpuAcceleration = true;
                
                // Configure window properties
                options.DefaultWindowWidth = 800;
                options.DefaultWindowHeight = 600;
            })
            .ConfigureFonts(fonts =>
            {
                fonts.AddFont("OpenSans-Regular.ttf", "OpenSansRegular");
                fonts.AddFont("OpenSans-Semibold.ttf", "OpenSansSemibold");
            });

        return builder.Build();
    }
}
```

The `UseLinuxPlatform()` extension method configures OpenMaui-specific settings including display server selection, GPU acceleration, and window defaults.

### Exploring MainPage.xaml

The default page demonstrates several OpenMaui controls:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyFirstMauiApp.MainPage">

    <ScrollView>
        <VerticalStackLayout Spacing="25" Padding="30">
            <Image Source="dotnet_bot.png"
                   HeightRequest="185"
                   Aspect="AspectFit" />

            <Label Text="Hello, OpenMaui!"
                   FontSize="32"
                   HorizontalOptions="Center" />

            <Label Text="Welcome to .NET MAUI on Linux"
                   FontSize="18"
                   HorizontalOptions="Center" />

            <Button Text="Click me"
                    Clicked="OnCounterClicked"
                    HorizontalOptions="Center" />

            <Label x:Name="CounterLabel"
                   Text="Current count: 0"
                   HorizontalOptions="Center" />
        </VerticalStackLayout>
    </ScrollView>
</ContentPage>
```

### Building and Running

Restore dependencies and build the project:

```bash
dotnet restore
dotnet build
```

Run your first OpenMaui application:

```bash
dotnet run
```

You should see a window appear with the sample UI. Click the button to increment the counter — you're now running a native .NET MAUI application on Linux!

### Troubleshooting Common Issues

**Issue: "Could not load SkiaSharp native library"**

Solution: Ensure libskia is installed and add the native assets:

```bash
dotnet add package SkiaSharp.NativeAssets.Linux
```

**Issue: Window doesn't appear**

Solution: Check your display server environment variable:

```bash
echo $WAYLAND_DISPLAY  # For Wayland
echo $DISPLAY          # For X11
```

**Issue: Build errors about MAUI SDK**

Solution: Verify the MAUI workload is installed:

```bash
dotnet workload list
dotnet workload install maui
```

Congratulations! You've successfully created and run your first OpenMaui application. In the next sections, we'll explore the project structure in detail and learn how to work with different display servers.
